title: Planet Fitness
html:
Planet Fitness

Planet Fitness shows up in the corpus in two distinct registers — once as a stock pick, once as a survival expense — and the gap between those two readings is one of the cleanest five-year arcs of voluntary adversity in the vault.

The first appearance is in 2020 04 23 go to diamond, April 2020, peak-COVID lockdown. 17-year-old Yahir, building his custodial-TDA watchlist alongside Beyond Meat, Trade Desk, Emerson Electric, and Hertz, is studying gyms as a value investing reopening play — the bet being that a low-cost-fitness brand survives the lockdown and prints when reopening hits. Planet Fitness is on the list with the implicit framing of good business model, beaten-down stock, asymmetric upside.

The second appearance is in 2025 05 30 todos and especially 2025 09 23 how im scraping by, where Planet Fitness is the demand-side compression line in his survival ledger. The note's exact language is "no new gym, $15 a month planet still" — Planet Fitness is the gym he refuses to upgrade from in his first weeks in Austin, part of the same belt-tightening that includes barber-school haircuts, $1.50/lb chicken thighs, and "I just got mad at myself mentally for spending $5.50 on a blended Horchata." He works overnight shifts at a different gym as primary bridge income (per Memory), so Planet Fitness is deliberately separate — the cheap personal membership versus the workplace-gym. The split is meaningful: he's running two gym relationships in parallel, one as labor, one as expense floor.

The five-year arc — from the April-2020 Munger-quoting watchlist entry to the Sep-2025 "$15 a month planet still" survival line — is a clean specimen of how Yahir's analytical interests become his lived constraints. The brand he was modeling as a public-equity reopening play in 2020 became the brand whose minimum-priced membership keeps him solvent in 2025. He correctly priced the asymmetry of low-cost gyms; he just didn't know he'd be on the consumer side of the ledger.
